sentences.text: Joseph Gringlas.
G-R-I-N-G-L-A-S.
No, it was a different spelling-- instead of 'I' was a 'Y.'
No.
No.
Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski, and it's in Poland.
O-S-T-R-O-W-I-E-C and Swietokrzyski, S-W-capital K-C-Y-S-K-I.
My mother's name was Blima.
Her maiden name was Fleishman.
My father was Leizor [INAUDIBLE] Aaron.
Yeah, I had-- we were five brothers and one sister.
The name of my oldest was Mendel, Shlomo, Jankel.
My sister was Beila, and there was Sol.
And I was the youngest, Joseph.
All boys and one girl.
That's right.
I grew up in my hometown in Poland, the same town what I told you before.
Yeah, they are originally from the same town.
How did parents meet?
[LAUGHS] I couldn't tell you that.
They never-- never told me about it, and-- I don't know.
They met-- this was not a big city.
Probably-- probably through family, through acquaintance, or something.
I couldn't tell you.
It was a farming-- farming and outside the city.
But down in the city was a-- was a big factory.
They built trains-- not passenger trains, commercial trains for loading things, you know, for shipment.
And it had a [? hot ?] oven.
There was like a-- they-- makes metal for-- metal for building trains.
It built all kinds of metal.
Anyway, in the same, there's a big oven.
They use all kinds of equipment for the oven to bring the metal to liquid.
It's a liquid.
So that's what place I worked during the war.
He did sell shoes.
He used to go to big city, capital city of Warsaw.
Brought it to my hometown, Ostrowiec.
He used to have in the market, selling shoes.
It was twice a week, Monday and Thursday, went to market for selling the shoes.
He was interested in be dressed up nice, holidays going to synagogue, and belonging to people, talking, and all kinds-- reading paper, newspapers, all kinds of stories.
Yeah, we were involved in praying, you know.
We were members in a synagogue, you know.
And I used to go every Saturday.
My father-- I was the youngest one, so I used to have to go.
So I went with my father to synagogue.
This was an old synagogue.
There was only one synagogue in that town.
It was built a few-- I was told it was built a few hundred years ago.
And it was destroyed after the Germans came in.
They burned it.
No, that was only-- only big-- there was other little, smaller ones, but this was the biggest in the city.
The rabbi, he-- he was known as Chaskiel.
And that was a-- I remember about him, his father was very big.
He was known over Poland.
He was such a-- I mean, now, it's [INAUDIBLE] and everything that [INAUDIBLE].
And my-- one thing I remember, my father, when he used to go to Warsaw to send special challahs-- you know the baked challahs for Shabbas?
They sent them with him for the rabbi, which my father told me about it.
But I-- at my-- at my time, I knew that was his son, because the father wasn't living anymore.
His son, the rabbi was-- his name was Chaskiel.
And to look at that rabbi was something like you look at a god, you know?
He was dressed in a big hat, fur hat, and long, black things.
It's different than you see here in this country, [LAUGHS] [? always. ?] He looked really different, you know-- very old-time.
What was it like for you when you went to synagogue with your father?
What did that feel like, and what was it like when you went in the door there?
It was a happy time to be with my father.
And I-- I was very close to my father, because I was the youngest son, so always close.
Even when he went to Warsaw for shoes.
In a time when-- when I was out of school, in vacation summertime, so we-- I went with him a few times to Warsaw and see how things going in business.
She was a housewife, really took care of the family, like, you know, if you have big family.
So she was always busy cooking, cleaning, washing.
And took care of us very, taking care of the way it should be taken care.
And even when I-- you know, I used to go to cheder, you know.
And she was very caring about.
In summertime, you know, I couldn't go back to home.
She would bring food to me and hide it.
So those things you never forget.
I was a little boy.
So she was very good mother.
I had good parents, lovely parents.
Did she have a lot of friends or family in the community?
Yeah, she had two sisters living in-- in the same town and had friends, you know.
And, you know, they went to shul, synagogue, and they met family there all the time.
So-- and we used to go to the other-- my aunt's family for the holidays and see the cousins and everybody else.
Oh, it was different celebrated as here.
It was-- like before Passover, they used to be putting in piece of bread in the evening.
And before the day of Passover, used to take-- clean it up, bread, and put it together with a wooden spoon.
And they would-- the next morning, they would burn it outside.
It had to be burned.
This was cleaning up from the bread, [? would ?] coming to starting eating matzahs.
So that was, I remember, all the time.
Oh, yeah.
I was closer to my brother which is a lot older from me, because we played soccer all the time with other friends.
He was only four, four and a half years different from me.
So I always played soccer in this-- with all other friends from the [? main ?] town.
Sol.
And he's the only survivor from my family.
Yeah.
I remember they used to come, friends, to my older brothers, and sister.
We used to have parties when-- and they danced.
And I was, at that time, about 9, 10 years old.
And I-- I really paid attention to how they were doing the dance.
So I would-- got into me, and I was dancing.
I remember my sister used to go to a party, so I was about 10 years, 11 years old.
And-- and I danced with her, like one of the youngest ones, because I love dancing.
So it was-- it was always a party.
It was always friends coming to the family.
It was always happy feeling.
Yeah, very strong Jewish community.
Very strong.
You know, like I mentioned, went to cheder.
And then after cheder, went to public school.
So it was very strong.
Yeah.
I remember we had, as Jewish kids, used to have a religious teacher at religious hour.
We went with our religious teacher, and-- and other kids went with the-- with the [? father, ?] the Christians.
So we had separated that time.
I'm remembering very good.
I remember exactly what we learned about all history about Jewish history and-- and religion.
Did you ever experience any anti-Semitism as a child?
Yes, I experienced a lot of anti-Semitic experiences.
When I went to cheder-- and at that time, when I went home, and at the time, the church was not far from the market where the cheder was.
I was always hit in-- I was hit in the back.
Somebody would run into me and was hitting me.
I always came up crying.
I was hit, and I didn't know why, what reason I was hit.
But that was anti-Semitic.
And other things-- I remember in the summertime, there was a-- a lake at our town.
And we used to go swimming.
And it was separate.
They separated us.
We couldn't swim with the Gentiles in same-- same river.
So we had, like, a Jewish special place to go.
And a lot of times, in there, they were throwing stones at us when we went through to get lake.
So it was very bad experience, yeah.
It was terrible that we lived, and we have to take it.
We can't do, because-- because there was-- nobody would help us to-- to clear this up and to do something for it.
Because it's-- it's in that-- and the people [INAUDIBLE] in that anti-Semitic things that they-- what they couldn't do anything about it.
So they'd be very, like, accustomed to it.
It's supposed to be like that.
Yeah, we had friends-- nice, very nice people and weren't Jewish, yeah.
Very, very close friends, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, by the way, I was-- where we lived was Gentiles.
The landlords were the Gentiles.
And I remember the one-- one daughter was my age and went to school.
And her parents were anti-Semitic, but she was not.
And she spoke Jewish like-- like us.
She was very close.
Shows you that-- usually, they think the parents anti-Semitic, the kids would be.
But this was exceptional.
She was very nice.
Saturday, I didn't go to school, so Sunday day, she told me all the lessons what they had to do for next week.
Very close.
Hannah, ?] Ana, yeah.
And her last name was Rogofska, R-O-G-O-F-S-K-I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a lot of other friends, but she was in the same-- living the same apartment where we lived, so was very close.
It was-- it was-- [LAUGHS] it wasn't like kitchens and bedrooms.
It was one big place, that's all.
It was just stove [INAUDIBLE].
We washed ourselves-- it wasn't like a bathroom.
We had a big, wooden things, I mean-- everyone had to go in and wash yourself.
That was different.
It's-- wasn't like here.
There were some, probably, other rich people, like lawyers or doctors, that had a little better-- better living.
And I think they had better things in life.
But the average was hard life.
Oh, yeah, there were a lot of--
Oh, yeah.
There were doctors allowed, Jewish professionals, yeah.
But for example, to go in a gymnasium, for us was-- we weren't able to do it.
Only those people with a lot of-- rich people could do it, go to gymnasium, or study higher schooling.
So the only thing-- public school was just-- was Jewish people.
That shows you how different, how they treat us.
I think the population was-- I think it was 30,000 altogether.
But it was 15,000, I think, Jewish people.
And it looked like-- the town was actually, like, Jewish, because the stores were all Jewish.
It looked like living in a Jewish-- but we were surrounded with Gentiles.
And the church, as I told you before-- I had to go to the market.
So-- and-- so but feel mostly we were around with Jewish people.
But the landlord what where we lived were Gentiles.
Yeah.
I grew up-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH], which means 'market.' And it was number 51.
Yeah, before the war started, we had already newspapers and listened to radios that the Germany trying-- how they propagandas against the Jewish people.
And how they-- they consider other people are lower class, and they're high-- higher morale, higher standard people.
And then, before the war broke out, we knew that something was filling the air.
Even I remember in school, geography, that one of the geography man-- the teacher said, 'See how the-- how the map is moving?
It looks like the war's coming.' That's what I remember he said.
That was just before the war broke out.
What did your parents say about these reports?
Did they talk with you about any of this?
Talk, but we couldn't do anything.
But I didn't-- didn't think-- believe it's going to come to something like this.
We thought, you know, we're going to be in trouble, but not something would happen.
Nobody in right mind could think about, it's going to happen like that, what happened.
Yes.
Yeah, we-- we experienced it, because we-- it first was starting with Czechoslovakia.
That was 1938.
And the Germans tried to-- wanted to get a part of Czechoslovakia.
At the time, I remember, I said-- I was very interested in reading paper.
And it was the chamberlain, the prime minister from England, went to Berlin and made peace at that time before the war.
[INAUDIBLE] And he came back to England, he said, 'We're going to have peace at our time.' But there was no peace.
There was a peace on the paper.
The war broke out a few months later.
I was 13.
I had just finished my public school.
I was 13 years old.
I remember when the war started, and a few weeks later when the Germans came in.
And I didn't know it was Germans, because England and France supposed to help Poland against the Germans.
So a marching soldier-- so I went to other side of the street and-- where they're marching, to the soldiers.
I saw the swastikas.
And you [? heard ?] exactly that they're Germans.
So that time, we started feeling that something really-- clouds is coming to us, something very bad things going to come.
And then what happened?
Right first day when they came in, they took out the doctors and lawyers, and they killed them out.
The Jewish doctors and lawyers?
Yeah, yeah, mm-hm.
Yeah, we saw them laying on the ground.
We heard it, and we saw it, yeah.
Mm-hm.
Probably, because it's a small town.
I mean, not small, but, I mean, it was 15,000.
So everyone knew everyone else.
And we heard about it.
People would-- saw it, and they told us.
And we knew that they weren't living anymore.
They were killed.
There was nothing could be done, because we were surrounded such a powerful army.
We think it is no place to go for help, no way.
I guess it was just the beginning.
Yeah, we were allowed to stay.
But they wanted [INAUDIBLE] before they took us to work, they need some for cleaning street of snow.
They went after the Jewish people, the young ones, to get us out in-- And at that times, they were hitting, and-- you know, and made you work, and screaming.
And it felt terrible.
That was the start of it.
He were not allowed to any do-- go any selling, doing anything, no.
He was not allowed to go out.
So only thing-- they gave us rations, piece of bread, a certain bread of certain amount.
I remember we had to stay in the back of the long lines to get the bread for the family.
This 19-- the war started in 1939.
This was continuing in the '40s, you know.
I beg your pardon?
Yeah, then there's-- they started-- it was not a yellow star.
We had armband, a blue-- a white armband with blue stripes, and a star of David was on the side.
And so that they knew exactly what-- who we are.
OK, I was about 14, 15.
Yes.
And there was warning everyday, find somebody, Jewish people or Jewish kids without their band-- they will be killed.
They knew that they already-- they're, like, separating us from other people.
Like trying to-- that-- for their-- you walk through, they know exactly what you are.
And they would do anything what they want to do with you.
They were worried, but they couldn't do anything about it.
You worry, but if you have something to do something about, you do it.
But what are you going-- if there's nothing you can do help yourself, what are you going to do?
But there was just-- we knew the problem.
But as I told you before, nobody could imagine what's going to happen.
How'd it start changing my life?
I mean, I felt very-- that we have no help, we're helpless.
That makes you feel like you can-- you're very getting, like, paralyzed.
You can't do free things what you want to do.
You want to work, you want to go to a movie, you want to go out.
You can't do that anymore.
And you have to be like a ghetto, they start to make.
You have to live all in the same place, same streets.
So it-- it was terrible feeling.
It was just helpless feeling.
Yeah, there was a ghetto in my hometown.
There was a ghetto.
They-- what the-- where we lived, at the address I told you, the ghetto was there.
It was close to the market, and a lot of Jewish people there.
But people lived out of-- out of the ghetto had to move in into the ghetto.
And people had to move with other families, because no place to stay, you know?
So that was the ghetto [? already. ?] They made this in there.
They surrounded it with, like, signs, 'This is the ghetto,' like the Gentiles shouldn't go in there.
Did you have other families at that time, then, move in with you?
At that time, there's-- yeah, there was other people in.

By that time already, Jewish people from close to the border of Germany that they already sent out, when the Jewish people-- when the Germans came in close to the German border, the Jewish people came to my hometown.
And I remember exactly there was [INAUDIBLE] a place, Konin, K-O-N-I-N, and that's around [?
Poznan ?] on the border of Germany.
So they shipped them out from there they lived-- where they lived.
So they came all, [?
Konin, ?] [INAUDIBLE] towns.
So I remember there was people living with my uncle and a lot of people in the same town, same ghetto.
Yeah, the ghetto was the name of my hometown.
No.
That's-- the only thing what they did is they take that who needed for work, they went in the ghetto, and they-- they took us out to go to work for the Germans.
And-- and the same time was, they made a Judenrat.
Judenrat means some oldest people to organize for them what they wanted to do something for the people.
Like they want people [INAUDIBLE] Judenrat, 'We need-- need 1,000 boys to work,' or something, so a few hundred boys [INAUDIBLE].
So they took us.
They had the order from them to do it.
Or they-- they want money.
They should get money out of the Jewish people.
And otherwise, they're going to kill us out, or something like that, they're warning.
So that's what the Judenrat was.
They did what they want, tell them what to do.
And then you had to do all the-- do things for them.
Did you know people who were in the Judenrat in your ghetto?
Oh, I mean, we know the people, yeah.
We know.
Sure, but you couldn't do anything.
We knew that they-- they have to do what they wanted-- told them to do.
And-- and they thought they were going to do it like going to work, giving what-- the money what they want, that's [INAUDIBLE] quiet them down.
So they did it.
Then, you know, they did-- they what they told them to do.
And they-- the Nazi told them, they did it.
What kind of work were you forced to do during this time?
I worked-- OK, cleaning, like snow cleaning, or something in the kitchens for the Germans for helping out.
And all kinds, you know, manual work.
Yeah, there's only-- with me was the older brother from me.
And-- but the other brothers were married, and they lived separate, anyway.
So they wasn't working.
They had to do something, helping out with themselves.
But they wasn't working with us two together.
They living in town, yeah.
But not with us, the ghetto, because there were different people.
Uh, yeah.
They took us to work the factory, you know, the factory I used to work, they call it putting trains-- the train, where they go-- they do, those metal sides for the train.
And we work on those train.
They'd start [INAUDIBLE] with that.
You know, wood, whatever was bad things, we have to-- for fix it up.
That's when they started working [? with ?] that.
That's where they got me in the-- the factory working.
And we know if anybody's going to work for awhile, then meanwhile, they're going to need us.
Then we'll-- we'll-- we won't survive, but for-- for a while, being interconnected, they need us, so they're not going to kill us out.
So that's what then happened when they start taking out all the Jewish people.
And I'll tell-- tell you what I told you about before.
I was-- let's see, that was in 1942.
I was about 15 years.
OK, there's only brother that older from me, Sol, worked with me.
And I told you about that special oven, they make metal.
And that's-- he was loading those, all kinds of material, for the oven.
And I was unloading big trains.
It's called cox, C-O-X.
It's something-- a material.
It's light but gives a lot of heat for the metal.
And I was unloading the trains.
And the trains was bigger than this house.
Unloading them, about two or three boys and a wagon.
They worked, yeah.
They worked with civilians, the other people.
Did some work.
There's only one.
The oldest one had a child, but-- but what happened during the-- they took us out in market.
Maybe I told you about this.
It was a Sunday.
And Saturday night, I worked at night shift in the factory.
So before I went to the factory, I looked at my parents, and thought, this is the last time I'm seeing them.
And my brother was working during daylight, so he was in the time when they took us-- took out the Jewish people all from all in the market to send them away.
So he was on the list from working with me in the-- the factory.
So he came down in the evening.
He told me that the oldest son was-- had a baby.
It was a bris, where the Jewish people-- they come to circumcise.
I didn't see him, but he saw it.
But that's-- then they had to go out.
And they made everybody get-- had to get out.
